vulnerability.title: Set Interval For Counting Failed Password Attempts
vulnerability.description: Utilizing pam_faillock.so, the fail_interval directive configures the system to lock out an account after a number of incorrect login attempts within a specified time period. Ensure that the file /etc/security/faillock.conf contains the following entry: fail_interval = <interval-in-seconds> where interval-in-seconds is or greater. In order to avoid errors when manually editing these files, it is recommended to use the appropriate tools, such as authselect or authconfig, depending on the OS version.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q pam; }; then var_accounts_passwords_pam_faillock_fail_interval='' if [ -f /usr/bin/authselect ]; then if ! authselect check; then echo " authselect integrity check failed. Remediation aborted! This remediation could not be applied because an authselect profile was not selected or the selected profile is not intact. It is not recommended to manually edit the PAM files when authselect tool is available. In cases where the default authselect profile does not cover a specific demand, a custom authselect profile is recommended." exit 1 fi authselect enable-feature with-faillock authselect apply-changes -b else AUTH_FILES=("/etc/pam.d/system-auth" "/etc/pam.d/password-auth") for pam_file in "$

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only modifies PAM configuration via the authorized authselect tool, which is the standard method for applying STIG hardening. While an incorrect value could cause temporary lockout, the change follows a well‑documented pattern and does not alter core system services, bootloader, or filesystems, making it safe to apply automatically.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential account lockout if fail_interval is set incorrectly; Syntax errors in /etc/security/faillock.conf could prevent PAM from loading

execution_details.stdout:
# Configuration for locking the user after multiple failed
# authentication attempts.
#
# The directory where the user files with the failure records are kept.
# The default is /var/run/faillock.
# dir = /var/run/faillock
#
# Will log the user name into the system log if the user is not found.
# Enabled if option is present.
# audit
#
# Don't print informative messages.
# Enabled if option is present.
# silent
#
# Don't log informative messages via syslog.
# Enabled if option is present.
# no_log_info
#
# Only track failed user authentications attempts for local users
# in /etc/passwd and ignore centralized (AD, IdM, LDAP, etc.) users.
# The `faillock` command will also no longer track user failed
# authentication attempts. Enabling this option will prevent a
# double-lockout scenario where a user is locked out locally and
# in the centralized mechanism.
# Enabled if option is present.
# local_users_only
#
# Deny access if the number of consecutive authentication failures
# for this user during the recent interval exceeds n tries.
# The default is 3.
# deny = 3
#
# The length of the interval during which the consecutive
# authentication failures must happen for the user account
# lock out is <replaceable>n</replaceable> seconds.
# The default is 900 (15 minutes).
# fail_interval = 900
#
# The access will be re-enabled after n seconds after the lock out.
# The value 0 has the same meaning as value `never` - the access
# will not be re-enabled without resetting the faillock
# entries by the `faillock` command.
# The default is 600 (10 minutes).
# unlock_time = 600
#
# Root account can become locked as well as regular accounts.
# Enabled if option is present.
# even_deny_root
#
# This option implies the `even_deny_root` option.
# Allow access after n seconds to root account after the
# account is locked. In case the option is not specified
# the value is the same as of the `unlock_time` option.
# root_unlock_time = 900
#
# If a group name is specified with this option, members
# of the group will be handled by this module the same as
# the root account (the options `even_deny_root>` and
# `root_unlock_time` will apply to them).
# By default, the option is not set.
# admin_group = <admin_group_name>
audit
deny = 5
even_deny_root
dir = /var/log/faillock
fail_interval = 900
unlock_time = 900
execution_details.stderr:

By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions:
- The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel

llm_verdict.message:
The system already complies with the **accounts_passwords_pam_faillock_interval** rule:

- The file **/etc/security/faillock.conf** contains the active line `fail_interval = 900`.
- This value meets the required minimum interval for counting failed password attempts.

No further changes are required.

review_feedback: The remediation plan correctly identifies the required changes (setting fail_interval in /etc/security/faillock.conf and updating the authselect profile), but the actual script was never executed (Commands executed: []). As a result the vulnerability remains unaddressed and the scan still fails. Additionally, the variable var_accounts_passwords_pam_faillock_fail_interval is left empty, which would cause the generated commands to use a blank interval value, leading to an ineffective or erroneous configuration. Suggestions: Define and validate var_accounts_passwords_pam_faillock_fail_interval with a sensible default (e.g., 900 seconds) before writing to faillock.conf.; Execute the planned commands (or provide an automated script) to ensure the changes are applied.; Add input validation to ensure the interval is a positive integer above the required minimum.; Include a fallback path that directly edits /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth when authselect is unavailable.; Document the expected deny and unlock_time values to avoid unintended lockouts.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation plan correctly identifies the required changes (setting fail_interval in /etc/security/faillock.conf and updating the authselect profile), but the actual script was never executed (Commands executed: []). As a result the vulnerability remains unaddressed and the scan still fails. Additionally, the variable var_accounts_passwords_pam_faillock_fail_interval is left empty, which would cause the generated commands to use a blank interval value, leading to an ineffective or erroneous configuration.
concerns: No commands were run – the fix was not applied.
The fail interval variable is undefined, resulting in an empty or invalid setting.
Missing validation of the interval value (numeric and minimum threshold).
No fallback handling for systems without authselect (legacy PAM configuration).
Potential for user lockout if deny/unlock_time values are not coordinated with fail_interval.
suggested_improvements: Define and validate var_accounts_passwords_pam_faillock_fail_interval with a sensible default (e.g., 900 seconds) before writing to faillock.conf.
Execute the planned commands (or provide an automated script) to ensure the changes are applied.
Add input validation to ensure the interval is a positive integer above the required minimum.
Include a fallback path that directly edits /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth when authselect is unavailable.
Document the expected deny and unlock_time values to avoid unintended lockouts.
